d.u: Emperor He of Southern Qi, Xiao Baorong

v.t: Xiao Yan rises in revolt, captures Jiankang; Marquis of Donghun, Xiao Baojuan, killed
v.s: Xiao Yan, the governor of Yongzhou in Southern Qi, raised an army in Xiangyang against the tyrannical rule of the Marquis of Donghun, Xiao Baojuan. Xiao Yan led his forces eastward, gaining support along the way. In March of the following year, Xiao Yan captured Jiankang (present-day Nanjing), and the Marquis of Donghun was killed by his own general. Xiao Yan then took control of the court and in 502 accepted the abdication to establish the Liang dynasty. This coup ended the rule of Southern Qi and began the 48-year reign of Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan.

v.t: Sassanian Persia and Eastern Rome resume war
v.s: King Kavadh I of Sassanian Persia, using the Byzantine Empire's refusal to pay for defense of the Caucasus as a pretext, again launched war against Eastern Rome. The Persian army invaded Byzantine Armenia and Mesopotamia, burning many cities. The war lasted several years, exhausting both sides, and a peace treaty was reached in 506.

v.t: Buddhism continues to flourish in northern and southern China
v.s: During the Southern and Northern Dynasties, Buddhism entered a period of peak development in China. In the south, Emperor Wu of Liang (Xiao Yan) was already a devout Buddhist even before ascending the throne; in the north, the Northern Wei court also strongly supported translation of scriptures and construction of temples. The number of Buddhist monks soared, and the monastic economy grew increasingly powerful. This period saw the emergence of early forms of several Buddhist schools, such as Tiantai and Sanlun, laying the foundation for the prosperity of Buddhism in the Sui and Tang dynasties.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Xiao Yan replaces Qi and proclaims himself emperor, establishing the Liang dynasty
v.s: After capturing Jiankang and taking control of the court, Xiao Yan accepted the abdication of Emperor He of Qi and officially ascended the throne with the state name Liang, changing the era name to Tianjian – this was Emperor Wu of Liang. After ascending, he strove to govern diligently, reformed abuses, reduced punishments, and appointed capable officials, leading to a rapid recovery of Liang's national strength and a stable confrontation with Northern Wei.

v.t: Emperor Wu of Liang promotes civil governance, revives Confucianism
v.s: After ascending the throne, Emperor Wu of Liang vigorously promoted civil governance, established the Five Classics Erudites, founded a national university, and restored ritual and music systems. He personally wrote Confucian works such as "Lectures on the Book of Changes" and "Questions and Answers on the Spring and Autumn Annals," and assembled scholars to compile the "General History." The Liang court soon experienced a cultural flourishing, producing literary giants such as Liu Xie, Zhong Rong, and Xiao Tong, who left behind immortal classics like "The Literary Mind and the Carving of Dragons," "The Critique of Poetry," and "Selections of Refined Literature."

v.t: Persian-Byzantine war continues
v.s: The Sassanian Persian and Eastern Roman empires continued fighting in northern Mesopotamia. The Persian army captured important strongholds such as Amida (present-day Diyarbakır, Turkey), forcing Eastern Roman Emperor Anastasius I to strengthen his eastern defenses by building the fortress of Dara. The protracted war severely drained the resources of both states.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Border conflict erupts between Liang and Northern Wei
v.s: Military conflict broke out between Liang and Northern Wei in the region of Yiyang (present-day Xinyang, Henan). Yuan Cheng, the Prince of Rencheng of Northern Wei, led an army to attack Zhongli, while Emperor Wu of Liang sent generals Zhang Huishao and Wei Rui to resist. The two sides fought a seesaw battle along the Huai River, with both winning and losing. This war marked the beginning of a long-term confrontation between Liang and Wei, which continued for decades.

v.t: Emperor Wu of Liang begins to favor Buddhism
v.s: In this year, Emperor Wu of Liang attended a Dharma assembly at Tongtai Temple (later renamed Jiming Temple), formally showing his strong interest in Buddhism. Thereafter he gradually elevated Buddhism to a near-state religion, many times giving up his body to the temple, building numerous Buddhist temples, and holding great assemblies. This policy deeply affected Liang's politics and society.

v.t: Byzantium and Persia resume hostilities
v.s: Large-scale fighting broke out again between the Eastern Roman Empire and Sassanian Persia in the region of Armenia. The Persian army breached Byzantine defenses and plundered several cities. Byzantine generals launched a counterattack, leading to a war of attrition that devastated the border areas.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Northern Wei captures Liang Province; Liang army retreats in defeat
v.s: The Northern Wei army, commanded by Xing Luan, launched a major offensive against Liang Province (around present-day Hanzhong, Shaanxi) of Southern Liang. The Liang defense crumbled, and various cities in Hanzhong fell one after another. After occupying Liang Province, the Wei further threatened Yi Province (present-day Sichuan), dealing a heavy blow to Liang's western defenses.

v.t: Frankish kingdom conquers Burgundy
v.s: Chlodomer, son of Frankish king Clovis I, led an army against the Kingdom of Burgundy. King Sigismund of Burgundy was captured and executed after defeat. Although Burgundian remnants under Gondomar briefly recovered later, Burgundy was completely absorbed by the Franks in 534. This conquest expanded Frankish territory, making it the most powerful Germanic kingdom in Western Europe.

v.t: Deepening Christianization of the Frankish kingdom
v.s: Since Clovis I's conversion to Catholicism, the Frankish kingdom under the Merovingians accelerated its Christianization. Bishops and monasteries became important pillars of the kingdom, and the church organization became closely intertwined with royal power, laying the religious foundation for the later Carolingian Renaissance.

v.t: Codification of Germanic customary law begins
v.s: Between the 5th and 9th centuries AD, the ancient customary laws of the Germanic tribes were gradually written down, forming the earliest Germanic law codes, such as the Salic Law (Franks), Visigothic Code, and Burgundian Code. Characterized by blood feud, wergild, and ordeals, these codes contrasted sharply with the rationalist tradition of Roman law and profoundly influenced the legal systems of medieval Europe.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Emperor Wu of Liang launches a large-scale northern expedition
v.s: Emperor Wu of Liang made Xiao Hong, Prince of Linchuan, commander-in-chief of a large northern expedition aimed at recovering the Huaipei region occupied by Northern Wei. The Liang army advanced in several columns, and its vanguard once captured Liangcheng (present-day Shou County, Anhui). However, Xiao Hong was cowardly and incompetent; upon encountering Wei reinforcements at Luokou (present-day Huaiyuan, Anhui), he fled, causing a rout and heavy losses. This failed expedition severely weakened Liang.

v.t: Wei Rui's great victory at Hefei
v.s: Despite the overall failure of the Liang northern expedition, the famous general Wei Rui achieved a great victory at Hefei. He captured Hefei and decisively defeated the Northern Wei army, killing or capturing over ten thousand. He was a strict commander who shared hardships with his soldiers, earning their deep loyalty. This victory temporarily halted the Wei southward advance and saved some face for Liang.

v.t: Major reconstruction of Constantinople
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Anastasius I undertook a large-scale reconstruction of the capital's infrastructure, including repairing walls, building aqueducts, and constructing reservoirs. He also expanded the harbor to promote trade. These projects improved the city's water supply and defenses, making Constantinople the most prosperous city in Europe at the time.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Battle of Zhongli – Liang army crushes Northern Wei
v.s: Emperor Xuanwu of Northern Wei, Yuan Ke, sent Prince of Zhongshan, Yuan Ying, with several hundred thousand troops to besiege the key Liang stronghold Zhongli (present-day Fengyang, Anhui). Emperor Wu of Liang ordered famous generals Wei Rui and Cao Jingzong to go to its relief. Wei Rui defeated the Wei navy with fire attacks and night assaults, and used flooding to destroy the Wei earthworks, ultimately smashing the Wei army. Wei casualties were enormous, and Yuan Ying fled alone. The Battle of Zhongli was one of the rare major victories of the southern dynasties over the northern, stabilizing Liang's Huai River defenses.

v.t: Visigothic kingdom converts to Catholicism
v.s: During the reign of King Alaric II, the Visigoths gradually abandoned Arianism and converted to Roman Catholicism. This conversion removed the religious barrier between the Visigothic rulers and the local Roman-Hispanic population, promoting ethnic integration and the stability of the kingdom.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Clovis defeats Visigoths, seizes Aquitaine
v.s: Frankish King Clovis I defeated Visigothic King Alaric II at the Battle of Vouillé, where Alaric was killed. Clovis then occupied large territories of the Visigothic kingdom in Aquitaine (southwestern France), extending his realm south of the Loire River. The Visigothic remnants retreated to Spain, and the Frankish kingdom became the most powerful barbarian kingdom in Western Europe.

v.t: Liang and Wei agree to truce
v.s: After the disastrous defeat at Zhongli, Northern Wei lacked the strength to continue large-scale southern campaigns, and Liang, exhausted by its own failed expedition and consumption of national power, did not wish to continue fighting. Envoys exchanged, and a truce was reached, temporarily ceasing border conflicts and restoring limited trade. This peace lasted for several years.

v.t: Emergence of feudal system in Europe
v.s: Between the 5th and 10th centuries AD, the social systems of the Germanic kingdoms merged with the late Roman large-estate system, giving birth to the feudal system of medieval Western Europe. Kings granted land (benefices) to great nobles, who in turn granted portions to lesser lords, forming a hierarchical system based on land tenure and personal dependence. The rudiments of this system began to appear during the Merovingian period.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Emperor Wu of Liang codifies rituals and music
v.s: Emperor Wu of Liang ordered Shen Yue, Minister of the Imperial Secretariat, and others to re-establish court rituals and ceremonial music based on the Confucian classics. Liang's ritual system absorbed the essence of previous dynasties and introduced innovations, becoming a model for the southern dynasties. At the same time, Emperor Wu organized monks to compile Buddhist ceremonial codes, promoting the sinicization of Buddhism.

v.t: Franks conquer remaining Visigothic territories
v.s: Frankish King Clovis I continued to push south, conquering the last Visigothic strongholds in southern Gaul. Although the Visigoths rebuilt their kingdom in Spain, Gaul as a whole fell under Frankish control, laying the foundations for the modern territory of France.

v.t: Sassanian Persia suppresses the Mazdakite movement
v.s: Sassanian king Kavadh I had earlier supported the Mazdakite sect, which advocated common ownership of property and religious equality, causing great social upheaval. Facing strong opposition from the nobility and Zoroastrian clergy, Kavadh I turned against the Mazdakites, executing their leader Mazdak and thousands of his followers, thereby restoring the dominance of Zoroastrianism.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Northern Wei expands Luoyang on a large scale
v.s: After Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei moved the capital to Luoyang, the city had grown to a huge size after years of construction. Emperor Xuanwu, Yuan Ke, continued expanding the palace and temples, and large-scale carving began at the Longmen Grottoes. Luoyang became one of the most prosperous international metropolises in East Asia at the time, attracting merchants and monks from the Western Regions.

v.t: Sassanian Persia conquers the Hephthalites
v.s: Sassanian king Kavadh I allied with the Turks to attack the Hephthalite Empire (White Huns) from east and west. The Persian army captured important Hephthalite cities in Central Asia and thoroughly defeated them. As a result, Sassanian Persia recovered lost territories and extended its influence to the Amu Darya River basin, but it also allowed the Turks to become the new dominant power in Central Asia.

v.t: Ostrogothic and Byzantine alliance
v.s: Ostrogothic King Theodoric the Great maintained friendly relations with the Eastern Roman Empire, and the two concluded a treaty of alliance. The Ostrogoths acknowledged Byzantine suzerainty, and Byzantium recognized the legitimacy of Ostrogothic rule in Italy. This alliance maintained peace in Italy and promoted the fusion of Roman and Germanic cultures.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Ostrogothic kingdom solidifies its rule over Italy
v.s: In this year, Ostrogothic King Theodoric the Great vigorously promoted the reconstruction of Italy. He restored the aqueducts and walls of Rome, protected classical cultural heritage, and practiced religious tolerance, respecting the Catholic faith. Goths and Romans lived in harmony under his rule, and Italy experienced a period of relative prosperity and stability.

v.t: Liang dynasty promulgates a new calendar
v.s: Emperor Wu of Liang ordered astronomer Yu Qian and others to revise the calendar and issued the new "Tianjian Calendar." This calendar was more accurate than previous ones, improving the intercalation of months and calculations of solar terms, which was important for agricultural production and official timekeeping.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Frankish king Clovis I dies
v.s: Clovis I, the founder of the Frankish kingdom, died in Paris at about the age of 45. According to Frankish inheritance tradition, his four sons – Chlodomer, Childebert I, Chlothar I, and Theuderic I – divided the kingdom equally, ruling from Orléans, Paris, Soissons, and Metz respectively. Although the kingdom was nominally united, it was in fact divided, sowing the seeds of future civil war.

v.t: First truce between Byzantium and Persia
v.s: Both the Eastern Roman Empire and Sassanian Persia, exhausted by war, reached a short-lived truce. They agreed to maintain the existing borders and cease hostilities. Although peace lasted only a few years, it temporarily relieved military pressure on the Byzantine eastern front.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Emperor Wu of Liang promotes Confucianism, establishes Five Classics Erudites
v.s: Emperor Wu of Liang issued an edict to select talents proficient in the Five Classics of Confucianism throughout the empire, established the Five Classics Erudites, and gave them generous treatment. He also personally lectured on Confucian classics at the National University, prompting a great flourishing of learning. Emperor Wu attempted to use Confucianism as the foundation of governance while accommodating Buddhism and Taoism, forming a distinctive cultural policy.

v.t: Liang and Northern Wei confront each other along the Huai River
v.s: After the Battle of Zhongli, Liang and Wei formed a long-term standoff along the Huai River line. Both sides built numerous military fortresses and stationed elite troops. The border area alternated between war and peace, with intermittent trade. This standoff lasted for decades until internal turmoil within Northern Wei broke the stalemate.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Empress Dowager Hu begins to interfere in Northern Wei politics
v.s: Emperor Xuanwu of Northern Wei, Yuan Ke, died, and the crown prince Yuan Xu ascended the throne as Emperor Xiaoming, only five years old. Empress Dowager Hu (the former emperor's consort) ruled as regent, effectively controlling the court. She was a devout Buddhist who massively built temples, but she was also dissolute and favored treacherous ministers, accelerating the decline of Northern Wei.

v.t: Byzantium and Persia negotiate peace
v.s: After years of war, the Eastern Roman Empire and the Sassanian Persian Empire formally concluded a peace treaty, stipulating mutual non-aggression and dividing spheres of influence in Armenia. The treaty temporarily stabilized the eastern border, allowing the Eastern Romans to concentrate on dealing with barbarian threats from the north.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Liang constructs the Fushan Dam
v.s: To attack Northern Wei, Emperor Wu of Liang adopted the suggestion of hydraulic engineers to build a dam across the Huai River at Fushan (present-day Mingguang, Anhui), in an attempt to flood the major Wei stronghold of Shouyang. Liang conscripted over 200,000 laborers, expending enormous manpower and resources to build the dam. The project was extremely difficult; it was repeatedly washed away by floods and rebuilt many times.

v.t: Major riot in Constantinople
v.s: A major riot broke out in Constantinople, capital of the Eastern Roman Empire, due to religious disputes and food shortages. The two major chariot-racing factions, the Greens and the Blues, clashed violently, and the imperial palace was besieged. Emperor Anastasius I was forced to make concessions to calm the situation. This riot foreshadowed the turmoil of the later Nika revolt.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Emperor Xuanwu of Northern Wei dies; Empress Dowager Hu formally assumes regency
v.s: Emperor Xuanwu of Northern Wei, Yuan Ke, died of illness. Emperor Xiaoming, Yuan Xu, was young, so Empress Dowager Hu formally assumed regency. She promoted her brother-in-law Yuan Yi, the eunuch Liu Teng, and other sycophants, while sidelining loyal members of the imperial clan such as Yuan Xie. She also lavishly built Buddhist temples, consuming enormous state wealth, leading to increasing political corruption and an empty treasury.

v.t: Fushan Dam collapses, causing catastrophic flooding downstream
v.s: The Fushan Dam, built at enormous expense by Liang, collapsed completely due to a sudden rise of the Huai River. The ensuing floodwaters inundated hundreds of thousands of hectares of downstream farmland, destroyed countless villages, and drowned hundreds of thousands of people. This engineering disaster was one of the greatest tragedies in Liang's history and greatly weakened the state.

v.t: Austrasia emerges as a Frankish kingdom
v.s: Theudebert I, son of Theuderic I and grandson of Clovis I, inherited the kingdom of Austrasia (eastern Frankish lands). He actively expanded his territory, defeating the Thuringians and Burgundians, making Austrasia the most dynamic of the Frankish kingdoms.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Emperor Wu of Liang extravagantly favors Buddhism, first "gives himself up" to Tongtai Temple
v.s: Emperor Wu of Liang held an assembly at Tongtai Temple and for the first time publicly "gave himself up" to the temple as a monk. The horrified court officials paid a large sum of money (one hundred million cash) to ransom the emperor back to secular life. Thereafter, Emperor Wu repeatedly gave himself up to Tongtai Temple, each time requiring the court to spend huge sums to ransom him. This obsession with Buddhism consumed enormous state wealth, causing increasing financial difficulties for Liang.

v.t: War between Eastern Rome and the Goths
v.s: The Eastern Roman Empire came into armed conflict in the Balkans with Germanic tribes such as the Ostrogoths and Gepids. The Goths crossed the Danube to plunder, and after hard fighting the Byzantine army drove them back. This campaign consumed many imperial resources and revealed the weakness of the Danube defenses.

v.t: Ostrogothic cultural achievements – architecture and mosaics in Ravenna
v.s: Ostrogothic King Theodoric the Great continued building palaces and churches in Ravenna. The mosaics of the Basilica of San Vitale depict Theodoric and his court and are outstanding examples of Byzantine art. These buildings combine Roman, Byzantine, and Germanic artistic styles, reflecting the unique cultural flourishing of the Ostrogothic kingdom.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Emperor Wu of Liang orders prohibition of alcohol and meat
v.s: Influenced by Buddhist precepts, Emperor Wu of Liang personally wrote a "Text on Abstaining from Alcohol and Meat," requiring monks and nuns to be vegetarian, and advocating that court officials use vegetables and fruits instead of animal sacrifices in rituals. From this point, vegetarianism became a tradition in Chinese Buddhism. Emperor Wu himself strictly observed the precepts, eating only vegetables and bean soup for each meal, making him one of the most devout Buddhist emperors in Chinese history.

v.t: Burgundy completely annexed by the Franks
v.s: The Frankish kings Chlodomer, Childebert I, and Chlothar I joined forces and completely conquered the Kingdom of Burgundy. The last Burgundian king, Gondomar, was killed, and the Burgundian territories were divided among the Frankish kings. The Kingdom of Burgundy thus ceased to exist, and the Frankish kingdom's borders expanded to the Rhône River basin.

v.t: Spread of Celtic Christianity in Britain
v.s: In the Celtic regions of Britain, the monastic system continued to develop. Monks in Wales, Cornwall, and Ireland maintained independent Celtic church traditions, differing from the Roman church in the calculation of Easter and tonsure styles. These monasteries became preservers of scholarship and culture in Western Europe.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Byzantine Emperor Anastasius I dies; Justin I ascends
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Anastasius I died, and the aged commander of the Excubitors, Justin, was proclaimed emperor – Justin I. Justin was a peasant by birth, illiterate, but skilled in military matters. He relied on his nephew Justinian to handle state affairs, beginning the Justinian dynasty.

v.t: Empress Dowager Hu of Northern Wei poisons Emperor Xiaoming (misdated; actually 524)
v.s: Some sources incorrectly record that Empress Dowager Hu poisoned Emperor Xiaoming this year; the event actually occurred in 528. Correction: Emperor Xiaoming of Northern Wei, Yuan Xu, was poisoned by his mother, Empress Dowager Hu, in February 528. She then proclaimed a newborn daughter as emperor (later replaced by Yuan Zhao), triggering the rebellion of Erzhu Rong.

v.t: Emperor Wu of Liang again gives himself up to Tongtai Temple
v.s: Emperor Wu of Liang again became a monk at Tongtai Temple, and the court officials again paid one hundred million cash to ransom him. This practice was believed to accumulate merit, but it severely affected the state's finances and administrative efficiency.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Temporary reconciliation between Eastern and Western churches
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Justin I attempted to reconcile with the Roman See and end the Acacian Schism. Envoys from both sides held talks and reached some compromise on Christological disputes, temporarily easing church relations. However, fundamental differences remained, laying the groundwork for the future Great Schism.

v.t: Erzhu Rong rises up and enters Luoyang
v.s: Erzhu Rong, the chieftain of the Xiurong tribe in Northern Wei, raised an army under the pretext of removing evil ministers and marched south to attack Luoyang. He defeated the forces sent by Empress Dowager Hu, entered Luoyang, drowned the empress dowager and the young emperor Yuan Zhao in the Yellow River, and massacred over two thousand court officials – the so-called "Heyin Incident." Erzhu Rong then installed Emperor Xiaozhuang, Yuan Ziyou, and monopolized power.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Erzhu Rong installs Emperor Xiaozhuang and takes control of Northern Wei government
v.s: After the Heyin Incident, Erzhu Rong installed Yuan Ziyou as emperor (Emperor Xiaozhuang). Erzhu Rong appointed himself Grand Chancellor and commander-in-chief of all military affairs, and his partisans filled the court. The central authority of Northern Wei collapsed; local strongmen rose in revolt; the aftermath of the Six Frontier Town rebellions continued; and Northern Wei entered the eve of disintegration.

v.t: Aksumite Empire at its peak in Africa
v.s: The Aksumite Empire in East Africa reached its zenith under King Kaleb. Aksum controlled Red Sea trade, allied with the Byzantine Empire, and conquered Yemen to protect Christians from persecution by a Jewish king. Aksum was one of the most powerful African states of its time, famous for its stelae and coinage technology.

v.t: Goguryeo and Northern Wei clash at the border
v.s: King Jangsu of Goguryeo sent troops to raid the Northern Wei border in Liaodong, plundering people and goods. Wei retaliated, and the two sides fought along the Liao River with mixed results. Goguryeo successfully resisted the Wei attacks relying on its system of mountain fortresses.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Liang and Northern Wei resume border trade
v.s: After a brief truce, Southern Liang and Northern Wei reopened border markets. They established trading posts along the Huai River, where Liang exported silk, porcelain, and tea, while Wei provided horses, furs, and handicrafts. Trade promoted economic exchange between north and south, but border conflicts still occurred from time to time.

v.t: Severe famine in Sassanian Persia
v.s: A severe drought in Sassanian Persia led to crop failures and a devastating famine. The imperial government was unable to provide relief; people were displaced, and even cannibalism occurred. The famine exacerbated social conflicts, and remnants of the Mazdakite movement became active again.

v.t: Buddhism officially introduced to Goguryeo
v.s: Through contacts with the Northern and Southern Dynasties in China, Goguryeo was exposed to Buddhism, and some nobles began to convert. Goguryeo built Buddhist temples in Pyongyang and invited Chinese monks to translate scriptures. Buddhism gradually spread among the Goguryeo upper class, blending with indigenous shamanism.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Emperor Xiaozhuang of Northern Wei kills Erzhu Rong
v.s: Emperor Xiaozhuang of Northern Wei, Yuan Ziyou, unwilling to remain a puppet, set a trap in the Mingguang Palace and personally killed the powerful minister Erzhu Rong. However, Erzhu Rong's nephew Erzhu Zhao immediately rose in revenge, captured Luoyang, and killed Emperor Xiaozhuang. The imperial clan and powerful ministers slaughtered each other, and the central government completely disintegrated.

v.t: Byzantium and Sassanian Persia sign a thirty-year peace agreement
v.s: After years of warfare, the Eastern Roman Empire and the Sassanian Persian Empire formally concluded a thirty-year peace treaty. They pledged mutual non-aggression and to jointly maintain stability in the Caucasus. This peace allowed Byzantium to concentrate fully on dealing with barbarian threats in the west.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Outbreak of the Six Frontier Town rebellions in Northern Wei
v.s: The garrison households of the six northern towns of Northern Wei (Woye, Huaishuo, Wuchuan, Fuming, Rouxuan, Huaihuang), suffering from unequal treatment and spreading famine, launched a major rebellion under the leadership of Polihan Baling. The rebels quickly captured many garrison towns. The Northern Wei court sent troops to suppress them but suffered repeated defeats. The Six Frontier Town rebellions were the trigger for the fall of Northern Wei, igniting a nationwide turmoil.

v.t: Himyarite kingdom in Yemen adopts Judaism
v.s: The king of the Himyarite kingdom in southern Arabia, Dhu Nuwas, announced his conversion to Judaism and persecuted local Christians. He captured Najran and massacred the Christian population, an event that provoked a strong reaction from the Aksumite Empire and the Byzantine Empire.

v.t: Jerome's Latin Bible becomes widely circulated
v.s: St. Jerome's Latin Vulgate Bible came into widespread use in the Western church. Translated from the Hebrew and Greek originals, this Bible became the standard Bible version in medieval Europe, profoundly influencing Christian theology and Western culture.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Six Frontier Town rebellions expand; Northern Wei crisis deepens
v.s: Polihan Baling's rebel army defeated the imperial forces under Yuan Yuan, expanding its control over the entire northern frontier. Northern Wei was forced to request the help of the Rouran Khaganate to suppress the rebellion. The Rouran khagan, Anagui, led his army south and joined with Wei forces to crush the rebels. Polihan Baling was killed in battle. However, remnants fled to Hebei and continued to resist.

v.t: Philosopher Boethius executed by the Ostrogothic king
v.s: The Roman philosopher and statesman Boethius, accused of conspiring with the Eastern Roman Empire to restore Roman rule, was ordered executed by the Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great. In prison, Boethius wrote "The Consolation of Philosophy," which became the most popular philosophical work of the Middle Ages. His death marked the decline of classical Roman culture in Italy.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Dionysius Exiguus creates the Anno Domini system
v.s: The Roman monk Dionysius Exiguus proposed a dating system based on the birth of Jesus – the Anno Domini (AD) system. He calculated that Jesus was born in the 753rd year after the founding of Rome and designated that year as AD 1. This dating method was later adopted by the Church and gradually became the international standard.

v.t: Justinian designated as successor
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Justin I, having no sons of his own, designated his nephew Justinian as co-emperor and successor. Justinian began to participate in state affairs, preparing for his eventual assumption of full power and the launching of major wars of reconquest.

v.t: Northern Wei uses Rouran troops to suppress rebellions
v.s: The Northern Wei court again requested the Rouran Khaganate to send troops to help suppress the remaining rebel forces in Hebei. The Rouran cavalry plundered Hebei on a large scale, causing great destruction. Although the rebellion was temporarily suppressed, the northern part of Wei was left in ruins, the treasury empty, and the frontier garrisons depleted.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great dies
v.s: The great Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great died in Ravenna at about the age of seventy. He had ruled Italy for over thirty years, maintaining peaceful coexistence between Romans and Goths. His young grandson Athalaric succeeded him, with his mother Amalasuintha as regent. The Ostrogothic kingdom subsequently declined.

v.t: Ge Rong leads the Hebei rebel army
v.s: Remnants of the Six Frontier Town rebellions regrouped under Ge Rong and launched a larger uprising in Hebei. Ge Rong proclaimed himself Son of Heaven and established a state named Qi, commanding several hundred thousand followers and controlling a large area of Hebei, posing a serious threat to the Northern Wei capital Luoyang.

v.t: Ostrogothic-Byzantine relations deteriorate
v.s: After the death of Theodoric the Great, the new Ostrogothic king was a child, and the regent, Queen Amalasuintha, tried to maintain friendly relations with the Eastern Roman Empire. But the Byzantine emperor Justin I adopted a hardline stance toward the Ostrogoths, and relations gradually worsened, setting the stage for the later Gothic War.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Justinian I becomes Eastern Roman emperor
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Justin I died, and Justinian I became sole emperor, beginning his thirty-eight-year glorious reign. Justinian aspired to restore the former glory of the Roman Empire, undertaking extensive legal codification, military reconquests, and architectural projects. He is one of the most famous Byzantine emperors.

v.t: Emperor Wu of Liang gives himself up to Tongtai Temple for the third time
v.s: Emperor Wu of Liang again became a monk at Tongtai Temple, and the court again spent one hundred million cash to ransom him. By this time, the Buddhist monastic economy had severely eroded state finances; officials were corrupt, and military preparedness was neglected, but Emperor Wu, deeply immersed in Buddhism, remained oblivious.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Erzhu Rong defeats Ge Rong and pacifies the Hebei rebellion
v.s: Erzhu Rong, the powerful minister of Northern Wei, led 70,000 elite cavalry to a decisive battle against Ge Rong's several hundred thousand rebel troops near Yecheng. Using ambushes and shock tactics, Erzhu Rong smashed the rebel army, capturing and killing Ge Rong. The Hebei rebellion was suppressed, but the territory of Northern Wei was already riddled with wounds.

v.t: Iberian revolt against Sassanian Persia
v.s: The kingdom of Iberia in the Caucasus (present-day Georgia), with Byzantine support, launched a revolt against Sassanian Persia. The Persian king Kavadh I led an army to suppress it but was unable to fully control the region. Iberia became a long-term focus of Byzantine-Persian rivalry.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Justinian closes the Academy of Athens
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Justinian I ordered the closing of Plato's Academy in Athens, which had been founded in 387 BC and had lasted nearly a thousand years. Justinian believed that the pagan philosophy taught there conflicted with Christian doctrine. Seven professors, carrying precious manuscripts, fled to the Persian Empire. The classical Greek philosophical tradition temporarily ended in Western Europe, but its legacy continued in Persia and Syria.

v.t: St. Benedict establishes the Abbey of Monte Cassino
v.s: The Italian monk Benedict of Nursia founded a monastery on Monte Cassino and promulgated the "Rule of Saint Benedict," emphasizing "prayer and work." This rule became the model for Western monasticism. Benedictine monasteries became centers of cultural preservation in the Middle Ages, copying manuscripts, developing agriculture, and providing education, with far-reaching influence.

v.t: Chen Qingzhi's northern expedition enters Luoyang
v.s: Emperor Wu of Liang sent the famous general Chen Qingzhi to escort the Northern Wei prince Yuan Hao north to contend for the throne. Chen Qingzhi, with only 7,000 "white-robed" troops, repeatedly defeated the Northern Wei army and entered Luoyang, where Yuan Hao proclaimed himself emperor. However, Erzhu Rong gathered a large army for a counterattack; Chen Qingzhi, vastly outnumbered, was defeated and retreated to Liang. Although unsuccessful, the expedition became legendary for Chen's victories against overwhelming odds.

v.t: Promulgation of the Code of Justinian
v.s: The Codex Justinianus, compiled by the jurist Tribonian under Justinian I, was officially promulgated. This code collected the edicts of Roman emperors and was the first part of the Corpus Juris Civilis. It systematized Roman law and deeply influenced the civil law systems of later times.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Battle of Dara: Byzantines defeat Persians
v.s: The Eastern Roman general Belisarius defeated the Sassanian Persian army at Dara (in present-day Turkey). Belisarius, with a smaller force, used flexible tactics to inflict heavy losses on the Persian cavalry. This victory stabilized the Byzantine eastern frontier and raised Belisarius's reputation.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Gao Huan rebels against the Erzhu clan
v.s: Gao Huan, the governor of Jinzhou in Northern Wei, rose in revolt at Xindu against the domination of the Erzhu clan. Gao Huan had originally been a subordinate of Erzhu Rong; dissatisfied with Erzhu Zhao's tyranny, he struck out on his own. With the support of the Chinese gentry of Hebei, he allied with other anti-Erzhu forces and gradually grew stronger.

v.t: Yuwen Tai begins reforms in Western Wei
v.s: After taking control of Western Wei, Yuwen Tai implemented a series of reforms: establishing the fubing (territorial militia) system, implementing the equal-field system, and issuing the "Six Articles" to improve administration. These reforms strengthened Western Wei and laid the foundation for the later Northern Zhou unification of the north.

v.t: Babylonian Talmud completed
v.s: Jewish scholars in Babylon completed the compilation of the Babylonian Talmud. This monumental work contains the oral law of Judaism, rabbinic commentaries, and folklore, and is second only to the Bible as a classic of Judaism, profoundly influencing Jewish religion, law, and daily life.

v.t: Byzantium and Sassanian Persia conclude peace treaty
v.s: After prolonged warfare, Byzantium and Sassanian Persia reached a peace agreement, with Byzantium agreeing to pay a certain amount of gold in exchange for peace. This peace allowed Justinian I to concentrate his forces on recovering lost western territories.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Nika revolt suppressed
v.s: The Nika revolt broke out in Constantinople; the Blue and Green chariot-racing factions united against Justinian I, besieging the palace and attempting to depose the emperor. Empress Theodora urged resistance, and Belisarius and Narses led troops to massacre over 30,000 rioters, quelling the uprising. The revolt caused great destruction in the city but strengthened Justinian's rule.

v.t: Gao Huan captures Luoyang and destroys the Erzhu clan
v.s: Gao Huan defeated the allied forces of Erzhu Zhao at Yecheng and then captured Luoyang, eliminating the remaining Erzhu forces. He installed Yuan Xiu as Emperor Xiaowu and remained in Jinyang, controlling the court from afar, becoming the new powerful minister of Northern Wei.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Belisarius campaigns in North Africa, conquers the Vandal Kingdom
v.s: The Eastern Roman general Belisarius led 15,000 men on an expedition against the Vandal Kingdom in North Africa. He decisively defeated the Vandal army at the battles of Ad Decimum and Tricamarum, capturing King Gelimer. The Vandal kingdom was destroyed, and North Africa returned to Eastern Roman rule. This victory opened the series of Justinian's reconquests of western territories.

v.t: Code of Justinian formally completed
v.s: After four years of compilation, the revised Codex Justinianus was formally promulgated. This code was the core of the Corpus Juris Civilis and profoundly influenced later European legal systems, becoming an important source of civil law.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Northern Wei splits into Eastern Wei and Western Wei
v.s: Emperor Xiaowu of Northern Wei, Yuan Xiu, dissatisfied with Gao Huan's monopolization of power, fled to Guanzhong to join Yuwen Tai. Gao Huan installed another member of the imperial family, Yuan Shanjian, as emperor and moved the capital to Yecheng – this was Eastern Wei. Yuwen Tai then poisoned Emperor Xiaowu and installed Yuan Baoju as emperor with the capital at Chang'an – this was Western Wei. Northern Wei formally split, and the north entered a period of east-west confrontation.

v.t: Construction of the Hagia Sophia begins
v.s: Justinian I ordered the rebuilding of the Hagia Sophia in Constantinople, replacing the old church destroyed in the Nika revolt. He hired the mathematician Anthemius and the physicist Isidore to design it, determined to build a church of unprecedented grandeur.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Eastern Wei and Western Wei begin confrontation
v.s: Eastern Wei and Western Wei faced each other across the Yellow River. Both claimed to be the legitimate continuation of Northern Wei and fought constantly. Gao Huan controlled Eastern Wei, and Yuwen Tai controlled Western Wei. The two states warred for years, and the north fell into decades of division and warfare.

v.t: Gupta Empire disintegrates
v.s: The Gupta Empire in India disintegrated completely under continuous attacks by the Hephthalites (White Huns). Gupta territory was carved up by local governors and chieftains, and northern India fell into a fragmented state of small kingdoms. Buddhism's central position in India was gradually replaced by Hinduism.

v.t: Belisarius invades Italy
v.s: Justinian I, using the pretext of reconquering Italy, sent Belisarius to attack the Ostrogothic kingdom. Belisarius landed in Sicily and quickly captured Naples. The Gothic War began, lasting nearly twenty years.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Belisarius captures Rome
v.s: Belisarius entered Rome, and the Ostrogothic garrison fled without a fight. The city of Rome returned to Eastern Roman rule after sixty years. Belisarius then strengthened its defenses, preparing for an Ostrogothic counterattack.

v.t: Battle of Tongguan between Eastern and Western Wei
v.s: Gao Huan of Eastern Wei attacked the Western Wei stronghold of Tongguan. Yuwen Tai personally led the defense. The two sides fought fiercely at Tongguan, and Yuwen Tai used terrain to defeat the Eastern Wei army. This battle solidified Western Wei's defense of the Guanzhong region.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Battle of Shayuan – Western Wei crushes Eastern Wei
v.s: Gao Huan of Eastern Wei led 200,000 troops against Western Wei. Yuwen Tai, commanding only about 10,000 men, set an ambush at Shayuan (present-day Dali, Shaanxi). The Western Wei army, though vastly outnumbered, shattered the Eastern Wei forces, and Gao Huan fled in disgrace. This battle turned the tide for Western Wei, while Eastern Wei suffered heavy losses of its elite troops.

v.t: Ostrogothic king Vitiges besieges Rome
v.s: Ostrogothic king Vitiges gathered a large army to besiege Rome, attempting to regain control of Italy. Belisarius held the city with a small garrison, repelling repeated Gothic attacks using the city walls and his wits. The siege lasted a year, and famine broke out in Rome.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Belisarius lifts the siege of Rome
v.s: Belisarius took advantage of a diversion in the Gothic forces to sally out and attack the Gothic camp, while Byzantine reinforcements arrived. Vitiges was forced to lift the siege, and Rome was relieved. Belisarius then reconquered much of central Italy.

v.t: Battle of the River Bridge between Eastern and Western Wei
v.s: Eastern and Western Wei fought again at the Yellow River bridge. Gao Huan sent troops across the river to attack Western Wei, and Yuwen Tai led his forces to meet them. After several days of fierce fighting, the Eastern Wei attack was thwarted, and they were forced to retreat. This battle further drained the resources of both states.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Western Wei fully implements the fubing system
v.s: Yuwen Tai fully implemented the fubing (territorial militia) system in Western Wei, establishing the Eight Pillars of State and Twelve Great Generals, concentrating military power in the central government. The fubing system greatly enhanced the combat effectiveness of the Western Wei army, becoming an important military foundation for the later unification of the north by Northern Zhou.

v.t: Ostrogoths resist Byzantines
v.s: Under their new king, Totila, the Ostrogoths reorganized their forces and launched a counteroffensive. Totila used guerrilla tactics to gradually recover southern Italy, while the Byzantine-held territory shrank.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Sassanian Persians capture Antioch
v.s: Sassanian king Khosrow I, taking advantage of Byzantium's deep involvement in the Italian war, broke the peace treaty and invaded Syria with a large army. The Persian army captured Antioch, sacked the city, and carried off its inhabitants to Persia. The fall of Antioch shocked the Mediterranean world, and Justinian I was forced to reinforce the eastern front.

v.t: Jia Sixie authors "Qimin Yaoshu" (Essential Techniques for the Welfare of the People)
v.s: The Northern Wei agronomist Jia Sixie completed the "Qimin Yaoshu," the oldest extant agricultural encyclopedia in China. The work, in ten volumes and ninety-two chapters, covers crop cultivation, horticulture, animal husbandry, brewing, and more. It records advanced techniques such as crop rotation, use of green manure, and seed selection and breeding, profoundly influencing later Chinese agriculture.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Plague of Justinian breaks out
v.s: A devastating plague of bubonic plague began in the Egyptian port of Pelusium and rapidly spread throughout the Byzantine Empire. According to Procopius, Constantinople saw over 10,000 deaths per day. The epidemic recurred for nearly two centuries, reducing the Mediterranean population by approximately 25–50% and severely weakening Byzantine strength.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Totila leads Ostrogothic counterattack
v.s: The Ostrogothic king Totila reorganized his army, using guerrilla tactics to harass Byzantine supply lines, captured Naples, and then took Rome. Byzantine rule in Italy was in jeopardy.

v.t: Great plague sweeps the Mediterranean world
v.s: The Plague of Justinian reached its peak. Over 10,000 bodies were carried out of Constantinople each day; as graves ran out, mass graves had to be dug. Justinian himself contracted the disease but survived. The empire's military strength plummeted, tax revenues collapsed, and the project of revival suffered a severe blow.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Byzantium and Persia again agree to truce
v.s: After renewed fighting, Byzantium and Persia reached a temporary truce. Both sides, needing to recover from plague and other problems, agreed to maintain the status quo in Mesopotamia. But the peace was short-lived, and the aftermath of the 540 conflict lingered.

v.t: Totila captures Florence
v.s: Ostrogothic king Totila defeated the Byzantine army at Faenza and then captured Florence and other Tuscan cities. His just and merciful rule won the support of the Italian populace, making Byzantine rule in Italy increasingly difficult.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Hou Jing's rebellion erupts (actually 548)
v.s: This is a preview of a later event; the Hou Jing rebellion actually broke out in 548. The Eastern Wei defector Hou Jing, having rebelled, formed an alliance with Emperor Wu's nephew Xiao Zhengde, crossed the Yangtze, and captured the Taicheng (imperial palace) in Jiankang. Emperor Wu was besieged and starved to death, and the economy of the Jiangnan region suffered catastrophic destruction.

v.t: Su Chuo of Western Wei issues the Six Articles
v.s: Yuwen Tai, the powerful minister of Western Wei, ordered Su Chuo, Minister of Revenue, to issue the "Six Articles" – namely, to cultivate the heart, promote education, maximize land use, promote the virtuous, attend to lawsuits, and equalize taxes and corvée. This was the most important program of government for Western Wei, emphasizing the moral qualities of officials and care for the populace, and it laid the institutional foundation for Northern Zhou's unification.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Great plague spreads to Western Europe
v.s: The Plague of Justinian continued to spread into Western Europe, devastating Italy, southern Gaul, and the Iberian Peninsula. The Visigothic and Frankish kingdoms suffered massive population losses, labor shortages, abandoned farmland, and economic depression.

v.t: Turkic tribes begin to rise
v.s: The Turkic tribe, originally the blacksmith slaves of the Rouran, under their leader Bumin Qaghan, defeated the Tiele tribes and grew in strength. The Turks were skilled in ironworking and cavalry warfare, rapidly rising at the southern foot of the Altai Mountains. In 552 they would defeat the Rouran and establish the Turkic Khaganate.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Ostrogoths recapture Rome
v.s: Ostrogothic king Totila recaptured Rome after a year-long siege. He once considered razing the city to the ground but was dissuaded by his soldiers. The Roman Senate and classical traditions suffered irreparable damage in this war.

v.t: Battle of Mang Mountain between Eastern and Western Wei
v.s: Gao Huan of Eastern Wei and Yuwen Tai of Western Wei fought a major battle at Mang Mountain. Gao Huan initially heavily defeated the Western Wei army, and Yuwen Tai was nearly captured. However, the Western Wei general Dugu Xin counterattacked, and after heavy casualties on both sides, the armies withdrew. This battle further drained the strength of both states.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Gao Huan of Eastern Wei dies of illness
v.s: Gao Huan, the powerful minister of Eastern Wei, died of illness in Jinyang at the age of fifty-two. He had controlled the Eastern Wei government for fifteen years after defeating the Erzhu clan in 532. His sons Gao Cheng and Gao Yang succeeded him in power, and in 550 Gao Yang would depose the Eastern Wei emperor to establish the Northern Qi dynasty.

v.t: Hou Jing rebels against Wei and surrenders to Liang
v.s: Hou Jing, the Grand Military Commissioner of Henan in Eastern Wei, came into conflict with Gao Cheng and surrendered the thirteen prefectures of Henan to Liang. Emperor Wu of Liang, ignoring the objections of his ministers, accepted Hou Jing's surrender – a decision that directly led to the outbreak of the Hou Jing rebellion two years later.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Hou Jing's rebellion erupts
v.s: Hou Jing formally rebelled against Liang from Shouyang, allying with Emperor Wu's nephew Xiao Zhengde, and crossed the Yangtze under the pretext of "removing evil ministers." He defeated the Liang army outside Jiankang and besieged the Taicheng (imperial palace). Emperor Wu was trapped for four months; within the city, food ran out, and people died of starvation. The Hou Jing rebellion was a turning point in the history of the southern dynasties, dealing a devastating blow to the Jiangnan economy.

v.t: Belisarius recalled to Constantinople
v.s: Justinian I, displeased with Belisarius's progress in Italy and also fearing his growing power, ordered him to return. The Ostrogoths under Totila regained the advantage, and the Byzantine position in Italy became passive.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan

v.t: Hou Jing captures the Taicheng; Emperor Wu of Liang starves to death
v.s: Hou Jing's army stormed the Taicheng (imperial palace) in Jiankang; the city had long run out of food. Emperor Wu of Liang, Xiao Yan, was captured and placed under house arrest, and soon died of starvation and illness at the age of 86. Hou Jing took control of the court and installed Emperor Jianwen, Xiao Gang, as a puppet. The Liang dynasty existed in name only.

d.u: Emperor Jianwen of Liang, Xiao Gang

v.t: Gao Yang replaces Eastern Wei and establishes Northern Qi
v.s: Gao Yang, the second son of Gao Huan, forced Emperor Xiaojing of Eastern Wei to abdicate and ascended the throne, proclaiming a new state named Qi, known as Northern Qi. The capital was Yecheng, and it controlled Hebei, Shandong, Henan, and eastern Shanxi. Gao Yang was Emperor Wenxuan of Northern Qi; in his early reign the state was strong, but later it became corrupt and was destroyed by Northern Zhou in 577.

v.t: Hou Jing's army captures the San Wu region
v.s: After taking Jiankang, Hou Jing's army expanded into the San Wu region (Wu Commandery, Wuxing, Kuaiji), looting and killing, turning the fertile lands of Jiangnan into desolation. Many people fled south or died in the war, and the economic base of the southern dynasties was destroyed.

v.t: Lombards settle in Pannonia
v.s: The Lombard tribe, under their leader Alboin, settled in the Pannonian plain (present-day western Hungary) and destroyed the Gepid kingdom. They adopted the cavalry tactics of steppe nomads, preparing for their invasion of Italy in 568.

v.t: Tiwanaku and Wari civilizations coexist in the Andes
v.s: In the Andean region of South America, the Tiwanaku (near Lake Titicaca) and Wari (southern Peru) civilizations coexisted. Tiwanaku is known for its monumental stone sculptures and terraced agriculture, while the Wari built extensive road networks and administrative centers, laying the foundations for the Inca Empire.

d.u: Emperor Jianwen of Liang, Xiao Gang

v.t: Hou Jing deposes and kills Emperor Jianwen, proclaims himself emperor
v.s: Hou Jing deposed and killed the puppet emperor, Emperor Jianwen of Liang, and then ascended the throne in Jiankang, changing the state name to Han. He ruthlessly slaughtered members of the Liang imperial clan, provoking strong resistance from regional forces (Chen Baxian, Wang Sengbian). He reigned as emperor for only a year before being defeated.

v.t: Narses commands the Italian campaign
v.s: Justinian I appointed the eunuch general Narses as commander in Italy. Narses led a new expeditionary force from Dalmatia into northern Italy, changed tactics, emphasized cooperation between cavalry and archers, and allied with the Lombards. The Gothic War entered its final phase.

d.u: Emperor Yuan of Liang, Xiao Yi

v.t: Hou Jing is defeated and killed; end of the Hou Jing rebellion
v.s: Hou Jing was defeated by the joint forces of Chen Baxian and Wang Sengbian, fled by boat, and was killed by his own men. His body was taken to Jiankang and put on public display; it was torn apart and eaten by the populace. The four-year rebellion (548-552) left the southern dynasties in a permanent state of decline.

v.t: Establishment of the Turkic Khaganate
v.s: Bumin Qaghan, the Turkic leader, defeated the Rouran Khaganate and proclaimed himself Illig Qaghan on the Mongolian steppe, establishing the Turkic Khaganate. The khaganate stretched from the Greater Khingan Range in the east to the Caspian Sea in the west, from Lake Baikal in the north to the Great Wall in the south, becoming the largest nomadic empire in Eurasia at the time and controlling the Silk Road.

v.t: Battle of Taginae: Byzantines crush Ostrogoths
v.s: The Byzantine general Narses fought a decisive battle against Ostrogothic king Totila at Taginae. Narses employed a formation with infantry in the center and archers and cavalry on the wings, shattering the Ostrogothic cavalry. Totila was mortally wounded, and the Ostrogoths lost the strategic initiative.

v.t: Xiao Yi proclaims himself emperor at Jiangling
v.s: Xiao Yi, the seventh son of Emperor Wu of Liang, ascended the throne at Jiangling as Emperor Yuan of Liang. He was a master of literature, calligraphy, and painting, but his effective rule was limited to the Jingzhou area. In 554, Western Wei would attack and capture Jiangling, and before the city fell, Emperor Yuan would burn his collection of 140,000 volumes of books.

v.t: Buddhism officially introduced to Japan
v.s: King Seong of Baekje sent a mission to Emperor Kinmei of Japan, presenting a gilt-bronze statue of Shakyamuni Buddha and Buddhist scriptures – the official introduction of Buddhism to Japan. Buddhism clashed and then merged with indigenous Shinto; with the promotion of Prince Shōtoku and Empress Suiko, it gradually spread among the upper class, profoundly influencing Japanese culture, art, and architecture.

d.u: Emperor Yuan of Liang, Xiao Yi

v.t: Ostrogothic kingdom falls
v.s: The Byzantine general Narses decisively defeated the last Ostrogothic king Teia at the Battle of Sarno River; Teia was killed. The Ostrogothic kingdom (493-553), which had lasted about sixty years, was extinguished. Byzantium reestablished its rule over Italy, but years of war had left the peninsula in ruins.

v.t: Fifth Ecumenical Council at Constantinople
v.s: Justinian I convened the Fifth Ecumenical Council, condemning the "Three Chapters" in an attempt to reconcile the Orthodox and Monophysite factions. The council's decisions displeased the Western church and deepened the rift between the Eastern and Western churches.

v.t: Western Wei captures Yi Province
v.s: Taking advantage of the Hou Jing rebellion in the south, Yuwen Tai of Western Wei sent Wei Chijiong to capture Yi Province (present-day Sichuan). The Western Wei army captured Chengdu, and the acquisition of Yi Province doubled Western Wei's territory and greatly enhanced its economic and strategic depth.

d.u: Emperor Yuan of Liang, Xiao Yi

v.t: Xiao Yi burns 140,000 volumes of books
v.s: When the Western Wei army broke through Jiangling, Emperor Yuan of Liang ordered the burning of the 140,000 precious volumes in his palace collection, including classics, histories, philosophy, and belles-lettres, and many manuscripts. The Jiangling book burning was a great catastrophe in Chinese cultural history.

v.t: Western Wei captures Jiangling, kills Emperor Yuan of Liang
v.s: Yu Jin of Western Wei led 50,000 troops to capture Jiangling. Emperor Yuan of Liang surrendered and was executed. Western Wei plundered Jiangling, carried off tens of thousands of residents, and set up the puppet Later Liang state under Xiao Cha. The Liang regime collapsed entirely.

v.t: Justinian issues the Pragmatic Sanction for Italy
v.s: Justinian I issued the "Pragmatic Sanction," introducing the Byzantine legal system to Italy, restoring the property of Roman landowners and the church, and rebuilding the administrative system. Ravenna was established as the seat of the exarchate as the administrative center.

d.u: Emperor Jing of Liang, Xiao Fangzhi

v.t: Chen Baxian defeats Wang Sengbian, takes control of the Liang court
v.s: Chen Baxian launched a coup in Jiankang, defeating his political rival Wang Sengbian. Wang had surrendered to Northern Qi and installed a puppet emperor. Chen Baxian took control of the court, installed Emperor Jing of Liang, Xiao Fangzhi, and appointed himself Minister of the Imperial Secretariat, holding real power.

v.t: Lombards unify their tribes
v.s: The Lombard chieftain Alboin unified the various Lombard tribes, absorbed the fighting techniques of other Germanic and steppe peoples, and developed a military system centered on heavy cavalry, clearing the way for the invasion of Italy.

v.t: Northern Qi and Northern Zhou confront each other
v.s: Northern Qi, with the wealthy regions of Hebei and Shandong, was economically stronger; Northern Zhou, controlling strategic territories such as Guanzhong and Sichuan, grew stronger under the fubing reforms. The two sides fought repeatedly over Luoyang, Jinyang, and other places, engaging in a tug-of-war for more than twenty years.

d.u: Emperor Jing of Liang, Xiao Fangzhi

v.t: Yuwen Tai dies of illness
v.s: Yuwen Tai, the de facto founder and ruler of Western Wei, died of illness in Chang'an at the age of fifty. He had implemented the fubing system and the Six Articles, making Western Wei powerful. On his deathbed, he entrusted Yuwen Hu to assist Yuwen Jue, paving the way for Northern Zhou to replace Western Wei.

v.t: Byzantine expedition to Spain
v.s: Justinian I ordered an expedition to southern Spain. Taking advantage of civil strife in the Visigothic kingdom, the Byzantines occupied cities such as Seville and Córdoba, establishing the province of Spania and controlling the trade routes through the Strait of Gibraltar.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Chen, Chen Baxian

v.t: Chen Baxian replaces Liang, establishes Chen dynasty
v.s: Chen Baxian accepted the abdication of Emperor Jing of Liang and ascended the throne, proclaiming the state name Chen and changing the era name to Yongding – Emperor Wu of Chen. The Chen dynasty was the last of the southern dynasties (557-589). Chen Baxian was of humble origin and rose by military merit; he reigned only two years but stabilized the turbulent situation in Jiangnan.

v.t: Yuwen Jue establishes Northern Zhou; Yuwen Hu monopolizes power
v.s: Yuwen Jue, son of Yuwen Tai, with the support of Yuwen Hu, accepted the abdication of Emperor Gong of Western Wei and proclaimed the state name Zhou – Northern Zhou. Yuwen Hu appointed himself Grand Preceptor and Grand Chancellor, controlling military and civil power. He soon killed Yuwen Jue and installed Yuwen Yu as emperor.

v.t: Earthquake in Constantinople damages Hagia Sophia dome
v.s: A strong earthquake struck Constantinople, severely damaging the dome of the Hagia Sophia; part of it collapsed in May 558. Justinian I immediately ordered its reconstruction, using lighter materials and a more rational design and raising the height by about 6 meters, making it even more majestic.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Chen, Chen Baxian

v.t: Massive Slavic migration into the Balkans
v.s: Large numbers of Slavic tribes crossed the Danube and migrated south into the Balkan provinces of the Byzantine Empire. They established permanent settlements in areas devastated by war and plague, permanently altering the ethnic and linguistic map of the Balkans and laying the foundation for the distribution of modern South Slavic peoples.

v.t: Chen dynasty stabilizes Jiangnan
v.s: Emperor Wu of Chen, Chen Baxian, reorganized the army, pacified the populace, restored production, suppressed regional separatists, and promoted men of humble origin and talent, breaking the monopoly of the aristocratic clans, gradually bringing Jiangnan society back from the devastation of the Hou Jing rebellion.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Chen, Chen Baxian

v.t: Emperor Wu of Chen, Chen Baxian, dies of illness
v.s: Chen Baxian, the founding emperor of the Chen dynasty, died of illness in Jiankang at the age of fifty-seven. He reigned only two years but succeeded in stabilizing the Jiangnan situation after the Hou Jing rebellion. His nephew Chen Qian succeeded him (Emperor Wen of Chen), continuing the policies of stabilizing Jiangnan and developing the economy.

v.t: Avars invade the Byzantine frontier
v.s: The Avars, a nomadic people from the Central Asian steppes, began raiding the Byzantine Danube frontier. Known for their mobile cavalry, they demanded gold and land from Byzantium; when rebuffed, they repeatedly crossed the Danube to pillage, posing a serious threat to the Balkan defenses.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Chen, Chen Qian

v.t: Emperor Wen of Chen ascends the throne, reforms internal affairs
v.s: After ascending the throne, Emperor Wen of Chen, Chen Qian, abolished the heavy taxes and levies of the late Liang, reduced the burden on peasants, purged officialdom, punished corruption, recruited displaced people, and restored agricultural production. The Jiangnan economy gradually recovered from the damage of the Hou Jing rebellion, a period known as the "Tianjia Reign of Good Order."

v.t: Turkic Khaganate establishes diplomatic relations with Byzantium
v.s: The Turkic khagan Istämi sent an embassy to Constantinople, proposing an alliance with Byzantium against Sassanian Persia to break the Persian monopoly on the silk trade. This alliance promoted cultural and economic exchanges between East and West.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Chen, Chen Qian

v.t: Chlothar I reunifies the Frankish kingdom
v.s: Chlothar I, the youngest son of Clovis, reunified the Frankish kingdom after the deaths of his brothers, becoming the sole Merovingian monarch. He defeated the Saxons, Thuringians, and other tribes, but after his death the kingdom was again divided among his four sons.

v.t: Northern Qi and Northern Zhou clash at Luoyang
v.s: Emress Wu of Northern Qi, Gao Zhan, sent troops to attack Northern Zhou's Henan territory; Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou, Yuwen Yong, led a counterattack. The two sides fought fiercely at Luoyang, with neither gaining a decisive advantage, and both exhausting their resources.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Chen, Chen Qian

v.t: Byzantium and Persia conclude a fifty-year peace treaty
v.s: Byzantium and Sassanian Persia signed a fifty-year peace treaty. Byzantium agreed to pay an annual sum of gold to Persia in exchange for security on its eastern border, and they divided spheres of influence in Armenia and the Caucasus. Justinian I was thus able to concentrate on the western war.

v.t: Emperor Wen of Chen pacifies the rebellion in Xiang Province
v.s: In the third year of the Tianjia era (AD 562), Emperor Wen of Chen, Chen Qian, pacified a rebellion in Xiang Province (present-day Changsha, Hunan). During his reign (559-566), he worked to restore the Southern Chen's order in the middle reaches of the Yangtze, defeating challenges from regional military leaders like Hou Andu and consolidating Chen rule in the south.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Chen, Chen Qian

v.t: Northern Zhou allies with Turks to attack Northern Qi
v.s: Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou, Yuwen Yong, allied with Muqan Qaghan of the Turks to launch a joint attack on Northern Qi. The Northern Zhou army advanced east, while the Turkic cavalry moved south, aiming to take Yecheng in a pincer movement. However, the Turks withdrew early due to bad weather, and the Zhou army, left without its ally, was also forced to withdraw.

v.t: St. Columba establishes Iona Abbey
v.s: The Irish missionary St. Columba founded a monastery on the island of Iona off the west coast of Scotland, which became a center for the spread of Celtic Christianity in Scotland. From Iona, missionaries brought Christianity to northern England and the Picts of Scotland, and the monastery became an important center of learning and culture in the early Middle Ages.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Chen, Chen Qian

v.t: Battle of Luoyang – Northern Qi decisively defeats Northern Zhou
v.s: Emperor Wucheng of Northern Qi, Gao Zhan, inflicted a major defeat on the Northern Zhou army at Luoyang. Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou, Yuwen Yong, had sent troops to besiege Luoyang. The Northern Qi generals Duan Shao and Hulü Guang arrived with reinforcements and routed the Zhou army at Mount Mang, temporarily halting the Zhou advance east.

v.t: Justinian I falls into theological disputes in his old age
v.s: In his old age, Justinian I became deeply involved in theological disputes, attempting to reconcile the Orthodox and Monophysite factions by proposing the doctrine of the "incorruptibility of Christ's body," which aroused strong opposition from the church. He spent much of his energy on theological debates, neglecting the real crises facing the empire.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Chen, Chen Qian

v.t: Justinian I dies
v.s: The Eastern Roman Emperor Justinian I died in Constantinople at the age of eighty-three. In his thirty-eight-year reign, he left behind the Codex Justinianus, the Hagia Sophia, and the reconquest of the Mediterranean basin, but his overexpansion in his later years left the treasury empty.

v.t: Emperor Wucheng of Northern Qi abdicates in favor of his son
v.s: Emperor Wucheng of Northern Qi, Gao Zhan, abdicated in favor of his son Gao Wei (later Emperor Houzhu of Northern Qi), retiring as Taishang Huang (retired emperor). Gao Zhan was given to wine and women and political corruption; after his abdication, the Northern Qi government fell into the hands of sycophants, and the state rapidly declined.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Chen, Chen Qian

v.t: Emperor Wen of Chen dies; Emperor Fei of Chen succeeds
v.s: Emperor Wen of Chen, Chen Qian, died of illness in Jiankang at the age of forty-four. During his six-year reign, he brought the Chen dynasty back from the Hou Jing rebellion. His son Chen Bozong (Emperor Fei of Chen) was only eleven years old when he succeeded; his uncle Chen Xu served as regent and soon took control of the government, deposing Chen Bozong in 568 and establishing himself as Emperor Xuan of Chen.

v.t: Avars launch large-scale invasion of the Balkans
v.s: The Avar khagan Bayan I led his people in a large-scale invasion of the Balkan provinces of the Byzantine Empire, besieging important cities such as Thessalonica. The Byzantines were powerless to prevent it and were forced to pay a large sum of gold for a temporary peace.

d.u: Emperor Fei of Chen, Chen Bozong

v.t: Chen Xu eliminates opponents and monopolizes power
v.s: In AD 567, the regent and imperial uncle Chen Xu purged the Secretariat director Liu Shizhi and other opponents, gaining complete control of the court. Dissatisfied with merely acting as regent, he began to plan to seize the throne. In 569, Chen Xu deposed Chen Bozong and proclaimed himself emperor (Emperor Xuan of Chen), maintaining the rule of the Southern Chen dynasty.

v.t: Frankish king Sigebert marries Brunhilda
v.s: Sigebert I, king of Austrasia, married the Visigothic princess Brunhilda. His brother, King Chilperic of Neustria, married Brunhilda's sister, an unhappy marriage that sparked a hatred between the two queens, leading to a forty-year civil war within the Frankish kingdom.

d.u: Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu

v.t: Chen Xu deposes Chen Bozong and proclaims himself emperor
v.s: Chen Xu, acting in the name of the empress dowager, deposed the boy emperor Chen Bozong, demoting him to Prince of Linhai, and then ascended the throne as Emperor Xuan of Chen. He reigned for fourteen years (568-582). In the early part of his reign he was diligent and organized a northern expedition that recovered the Huainan region (in 573), marking the peak of Chen power.

v.t: Lombards invade Italy
v.s: The Lombard king Alboin led approximately 150,000 people across the Alps into northern Italy. The Byzantines, having just finished the Gothic War, had weak defenses and could not resist. The Lombards quickly occupied the Po River plain and established the Lombard Kingdom with its capital at Pavia.

d.u: Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu

v.t: Lombards capture Milan
v.s: The Lombard army captured Milan, the most important city in northern Italy; the archbishop and citizens were unable to resist. The Lombards established pro-Lombard ducal governments in Milan and the surrounding area, solidifying their control over the core of northern Italy.

v.t: Emperor Xuan of Chen rectifies the government
v.s: After ascending the throne, Emperor Xuan of Chen abolished the harsh policies of the late Emperor Wen, reduced imperial expenditures, strengthened central control over the provinces, and promoted capable civil and military officials such as Wu Mingche and Xu Ling. The Chen dynasty took on a new vitality.

d.u: Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu

v.t: Birth of the Prophet Muhammad, founder of Islam
v.s: The Prophet Muhammad, founder of Islam, was born in Mecca into the Hashim clan of the Quraysh tribe. His father died before his birth, and his mother died when he was six; he was raised by his grandfather and then his uncle. At the age of forty (c. 610 CE), while meditating in the cave of Hira, he received his first revelation from the angel Jibril (Gabriel) and began preaching Islam.

v.t: Division of the Turkic Khaganate begins
v.s: The vast Turkic Khaganate saw increasing conflict between its eastern and western wings. The eastern part was ruled by the descendants of Bumin Qaghan, the western by Istämi, and they were effectively independent. Conflict over succession to the khaganate led to a formal split in 583 into Eastern and Western Turkic Khaganates.

v.t: War between the Turks and Persia
v.s: Large-scale military conflict broke out between the Turkic Khaganate and Sassanian Persia in Central Asia and eastern Persia. The Turks allied with Byzantium and attacked Persia from both east and west, invading Khorasan and severely weakening the Sassanian Empire.

d.u: Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu

v.t: Northern Qi's famous general Hulü Guang is unjustly killed
v.s: Emperor Houzhu of Northern Qi, Gao Wei, heeding slander, executed the famous general Hulü Guang on charges of treason. Hulü Guang was a pillar in the war against Northern Zhou. His unjust death deprived Northern Qi of its most outstanding commander, accelerating its fall.

v.t: Avars invade Thrace
v.s: The Avar cavalry raided deep into Thrace, posing a serious threat to Constantinople. The Byzantine emperor Justin II was forced to mobilize troops northward and concluded a humiliating peace treaty.

d.u: Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu

v.t: Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou, Yuwen Yong, kills Yuwen Hu and assumes personal rule
v.s: Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou, Yuwen Yong, killed the powerful minister Yuwen Hu in Chang'an in a carefully planned trap. Yuwen Hu had monopolized power for fifteen years, deposing and killing two emperors. Yuwen Yong struck Yuwen Hu with a jade scepter in the empress dowager's palace on the pretext of urging her to abstain from alcohol, then purged his followers and began his personal rule, implementing a series of strong reforms.

v.t: Sassanian Persia conquers Yemen
v.s: The Sassanian king Khosrow I sent an army to conquer Yemen, establishing a governorate there and taking control of the incense trade routes of Arabia. Persian power extended deep into the southern Arabian Peninsula, indirectly influencing the later rise of Islam.

d.u: Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu

v.t: Emperor Xuan of Chen launches northern expedition, recovers Huainan
v.s: Emperor Xuan of Chen sent the famous general Wu Mingche with 100,000 troops on a northern expedition against Northern Qi. They captured Liyang, Shouyang, and other key cities, successfully recovering the Huainan region lost after the Hou Jing rebellion. This was the largest military operation of the Chen dynasty and the last major military victory of the southern dynasties over the northern.

v.t: Lombards expand into central Italy
v.s: The Lombard duke Faroald established the Duchy of Spoleto, controlling a strategic area east of the Apennines. The Lombards captured towns around Rome, seriously threatening the pope's security. Italy fell into a complex triangular political situation among the Byzantines, Lombards, and the papacy.

d.u: Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu

v.t: Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou persecutes Buddhism
v.s: Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou, Yuwen Yong, issued an edict to persecute Buddhism, forcibly abolishing the religion, destroying Buddhist temples, burning scriptures and images, and melting down bronze statues to mint coins, forcing monks and nuns to return to lay life. He argued that monasteries occupied vast land and people without paying taxes or providing corvée labor, severely affecting the state's manpower and revenues. The persecution effectively increased labor and military strength, laying an economic foundation for the unification of the north.

v.t: Tiberius II becomes Byzantine emperor
v.s: The Byzantine emperor Justin II, incapacitated by mental breakdown, designated the general Tiberius as Caesar to act as regent. Tiberius proved capable, stabilizing the empire and restoring defenses in the Balkans and on the eastern frontier.

d.u: Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu

v.t: Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou launches major expedition against Northern Qi
v.s: After completing the Buddhist persecution and the fubing reforms, Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou, Yuwen Yong, personally led a large army east to attack Northern Qi. The Zhou army captured strategic points such as Heyang, and its vanguard approached Yecheng. However, due to stubborn resistance from Northern Qi and supply difficulties, the Zhou army withdrew. This expedition allowed Yuwen Yong to gauge Northern Qi's strength.

d.u: Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu

v.t: Northern Zhou again attacks Qi, captures Jinyang
v.s: Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou again launched an eastern expedition, striking directly at the key Northern Qi stronghold Jinyang. Emperor Houzhu of Northern Qi, Gao Wei, led his army to meet him but fled from the battlefield; Jinyang fell, and the Northern Qi army collapsed. The Northern Qi emperor fled back to Yecheng, and the Zhou army pursued relentlessly.

v.t: Avars penetrate deep into the Greek peninsula
v.s: The Avar cavalry raided deep into the southern Greek peninsula, plundering and destroying many classical cities such as Athens. The inhabitants fled to islands or mountains, and the classical heritage of Greece suffered irreversible damage.

d.u: Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu

v.t: Northern Zhou destroys Northern Qi, unifies the north
v.s: In the first month, Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou, Yuwen Yong, led his army to break the Northern Qi capital Yecheng; Northern Qi's last emperor, Gao Wei, was captured, and Northern Qi fell. Northern Zhou united northern China, ending the forty-three-year east-west division that had existed since the split of Northern Wei in 534, and laid the foundation for the Sui dynasty to unify the whole country.

v.t: Political fragmentation of the Lombard Kingdom
v.s: The Lombard king directly controlled only parts of northern Italy; the southern duchies of Spoleto and Benevento were effectively semi-independent. This political fragmentation persisted throughout the Lombard period (568-774) and weakened the kingdom's ability to resist external enemies.

d.u: Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu

v.t: Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou dies of illness
v.s: Emperor Wu of Northern Zhou, Yuwen Yong, died of illness while on campaign against the Turks, at the young age of thirty-six. He had completed the unification of the north by destroying Northern Qi and carried out major reforms including the fubing system, the equal-field system, and the persecution of Buddhism. After his early death, his son Yuwen Yun (Emperor Xuan) succeeded, but he was dissolute and incompetent, leading to the rapid decline of Northern Zhou.

v.t: Tiberius II formally ascends the throne
v.s: Justin II died, and Tiberius II formally became emperor. He focused on fiscal economy, reducing redundant officials, concluded a peace treaty with Persia, and strengthened Balkan defenses. He was loved by the people of Constantinople.

d.u: Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu

v.t: Chen general Wu Mingche's northern expedition defeated by Northern Zhou
v.s: The Chen general Wu Mingche launched a northern expedition from Huainan, besieging Pengcheng (Xuzhou) in an attempt to recover northern Huainan. But Northern Zhou, having just destroyed Northern Qi, had grown much stronger. The Zhou general Wang Gui rescued the city and cut off Wu Mingche's supply lines. Wu Mingche was decisively defeated and captured, the Chen northern expedition failed, and the Huainan territories fell back into Northern Zhou hands.

v.t: Emperor Xuan of Northern Zhou misgoverns in debauchery
v.s: Emperor Xuan of Northern Zhou, Yuwen Yun, wallowed in wine and women, built lavish palaces, indiscriminately killed imperial clan members and ministers, and simultaneously established five empresses. He ignored state affairs, abdicated in favor of his young son Yuwen Chan (Emperor Jing), and lived as a retired emperor enjoying himself. The power of Northern Zhou rapidly declined.

d.u: Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu

v.t: Emperor Xuan of Northern Zhou dies; Yang Jian becomes regent
v.s: Emperor Xuan of Northern Zhou, Yuwen Yun, died of illness. The seven-year-old Yuwen Chan (Emperor Jing) succeeded. The powerful minister Yang Jian (father of the former emperor's consort) was appointed Grand Counselor and regent, taking control of state power. He quickly eliminated opposition from the Yuwen imperial clan, preparing the way for the Sui dynasty to replace Northern Zhou.

v.t: Yang Jian suppresses Yuchi Jiong's rebellion
v.s: Yuchi Jiong, the governor of Xiangzhou in Northern Zhou, raised an army against Yang Jian under the pretext of "removing evil ministers," controlling a large area of Shandong and Hebei. Yang Jian sent Wei Xiaokuan and Gao Jiong to attack him. They defeated Yuchi Jiong at Yecheng, the rebellion was suppressed, and Yang Jian's position was further consolidated.
