v.t: Slavs settle in the Balkans
v.s: Large numbers of Slavs crossed the Danube and settled in large numbers in the Balkan Peninsula. They destroyed the urban civilization and road systems of the Roman era and established settlements based on rural communes, permanently changing the ethnic composition of the peninsula.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian
Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu

v.t: Yang Jian usurps Zhou and establishes Sui
v.s: Yang Jian, a maternal relative of the Northern Zhou imperial house, forced Emperor Jing of Zhou to abdicate and ascended the throne, proclaiming the state name Sui and changing the era name to Kaihuang – Emperor Wen of Sui. He abolished the Northern Zhou six-bureaucrats system, restored the Han-style three departments and six ministries, promulgated the Kaihuang Code, and standardized weights and measures. The Sui dynasty became a powerful and unified state after the Qin and Han.

v.t: Succession crisis among the Turks
v.s: The death of the Turkic khagan Taspar led to a succession dispute. Some nobles supported Shètú (Shabolüe Qaghan), leading to a split and a long civil war. Emperor Wen of Sui, taking advantage of the Turkic infighting, adopted a strategy of "making distant allies and attacking near enemies, dividing and weakening."

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian
Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu

v.t: Turkic Shabolüe Qaghan's southern invasion repelled
v.s: The Turkic Shabolüe Qaghan led 400,000 cavalry to invade the Sui border, looting Wuwei, Tianshui, Yan'an, and other places. Emperor Wen of Sui adopted a strategy of fortifying and clearing the fields. Prince Yang Shuang and other generals led forces to resist, defeating the Turkic vanguard at Liangzhou. At the same time, he exploited Turkic internal conflicts to sow discord, causing the Turks to split into eastern and western parts, and Shabolüe was forced to withdraw.

v.t: Maurice ascends as Byzantine emperor
v.s: Tiberius II died, and Maurice became Byzantine emperor. He continued military reforms, strengthened the theme system, and maintained peace with Persia, bringing a period of brief stability to the empire.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian
Emperor Houzhu of Chen, Chen Shubao

v.t: Sui army defeats Turks; Turks split into East and West
v.s: The Sui army decisively defeated the Turkic Shabolüe Qaghan at Baidaochuan. Internal conflicts among the Turks intensified, and they formally split into the Eastern Turkic Khaganate and the Western Turkic Khaganate. Shabolüe of the Eastern Turks submitted as a vassal to Sui, while Tardu Qaghan of the Western Turks established his independence. Sui eliminated the northern threat by using a policy of divide and rule.

v.t: Emperor Xuan of Chen dies; Emperor Houzhu of Chen succeeds
v.s: Emperor Xuan of Chen, Chen Xu, died of illness, and his son Chen Shubao succeeded him – Emperor Houzhu of Chen. Emperor Houzhu was dissolute and ignored state affairs, trusting sycophants such as Consort Zhang. The Chen dynasty rapidly declined, presenting an opportunity for the Sui dynasty to destroy it.

v.t: Avars besiege Constantinople
v.s: The Avar khagan Bayan led his army to besiege Constantinople. The Byzantine defenders relied on the strong walls to resist stubbornly. The Avars could not break through and eventually withdrew after receiving a tribute of gold.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian
Emperor Houzhu of Chen, Chen Shubao

v.t: Turkic Shabolüe Qaghan submits as vassal to Sui
v.s: Under military and diplomatic pressure from the Sui dynasty, Shabolüe Qaghan of the Eastern Turks formally submitted as a vassal to Emperor Wen of Sui and accepted Sui investiture. Sui stabilized its northern frontier and was able to concentrate its forces in preparation for the southern campaign.

v.t: Sui excavates the Guangtong Canal
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui ordered Yuwen Kai to excavate the Guangtong Canal, diverting water from the Wei River from Daxing (Chang'an) east to Tongguan, connecting to the Yellow River. This was the beginning of the Sui dynasty's Grand Canal projects, improving transport to Guanzhong and providing logistical support for the unification of the country.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian
Emperor Houzhu of Chen, Chen Shubao

v.t: Emperor Wen of Sui implements the equal-field system and tax laws
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui issued an edict implementing the equal-field system, stipulating that each adult male be granted 80 mu of farmland and 20 mu of mulberry land. At the same time, he introduced a tax system that reduced the burden on peasants and increased state revenue. These measures promoted agricultural production and laid the economic foundation for Sui's prosperity.

v.t: Sui destroys the Later Liang, a prelude to unifying the south
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui ordered Yang Su as commander to destroy the Later Liang regime (established by Xiao Cha), which had been dependent on Northern Zhou. With the fall of Later Liang, the Sui fully controlled the middle Yangtze, clearing the way for the destruction of Chen.

v.t: Maurice recovers the Balkans
v.s: Emperor Maurice of Byzantium launched a counteroffensive on the Danube line, defeating the Avars and Slavs and recovering parts of the Balkans, stabilizing the northern border.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian
Emperor Houzhu of Chen, Chen Shubao

v.t: Sui carries out a nationwide census and registration reform
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui ordered a nationwide "Great Census" (da suo mao yue), i.e., checking household registers against actual persons and investigating concealed and omitted households. A total of over 1.64 million previously concealed persons were discovered, increasing the number of registered households and tax revenue.

v.t: Civil war in the Lombard kingdom
v.s: After the death of Lombard king Authari, the dukes fought over the throne, leading to civil war. The nobles ultimately elected Agilulf as king; he married the former queen Theodelinda and restored stability.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian
Emperor Houzhu of Chen, Chen Shubao

v.t: Sui destroys the Later Liang, fully unifies the north
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui sent Yang Su to wipe out the remaining forces of the Later Liang, incorporating Jiangling into the Sui domain. By now, the Sui had unified the entire north and the middle and upper Yangtze, forming a strategic encirclement of the Chen dynasty.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian
Emperor Houzhu of Chen, Chen Shubao

v.t: Emperor Wen of Sui launches a massive campaign to destroy Chen
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui appointed Yang Guang (Prince of Jin), Yang Jun (Prince of Qin), and Yang Su (Duke of Qinghe) as commanders, dividing the army into eight routes with a total of 518,000 men to launch a massive attack on the Chen dynasty. The Sui forces advanced along the entire Yangtze from Sichuan to Jiankang, and the Chen riverine garrisons collapsed one after another.

v.t: Byzantium and Persia contend over Armenia
v.s: In AD 588, war broke out again between the Byzantine Empire and Sassanian Persia over the issue of Armenia. The conflict between Byzantine Emperor Maurice and Persian King Hormizd IV intensified in Armenia and the Caucasus. The Persian general Bahram Chobin led a counterattack against the Byzantines. The war continued until 591, when Maurice supported Khosrow II to seize the Persian throne and concluded a favorable peace treaty.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian
Emperor Houzhu of Chen, Chen Shubao

v.t: Sui destroys Chen, unifies the empire
v.s: The Sui army entered Jiankang; Emperor Houzhu of Chen, Chen Shubao, hid with his consort Zhang in a dry well but was captured. The Chen dynasty fell, and the Sui unified the whole country, ending nearly three centuries of division since the end of the Western Jin. Emperor Wen of Sui implemented the equal-field system, reformed the bureaucracy, and established the imperial examination system, laying the institutional foundations for the prosperity of the Tang dynasty.

v.t: Post-unification reforms of the Sui dynasty
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui extended the northern equal-field and fubing systems to the whole country, unified laws, currency, and weights and measures across the north and south. He ordered the destruction of the extravagant "Tiantai" and other southern Liang structures, suppressing the power of the Jiangnan aristocratic clans and consolidating national unity.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian

v.t: Pope Gregory I ascends
v.s: Gregory I was elected Bishop of Rome (Pope). He actively expanded the secular power of the papacy, sent missionaries to evangelize England, codified Gregorian chant, and reorganized church discipline. He was one of the founders of the medieval papacy and had a profound influence on the development of Western Christianity.

v.t: Sui suppresses rebellion of Jiangnan aristocratic clans
v.s: After the Sui destroyed Chen, the Jiangnan aristocratic clans, discontented with the Sui's strict rule, launched a large-scale rebellion. Emperor Wen of Sui sent Yang Su to suppress it, pacifying the various regions of Jiangnan. The Sui then adopted policies of conciliation, stabilizing the south.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian

v.t: Sassanian prince flees to Byzantium
v.s: The Sassanian prince Khosrow II, having lost in a struggle against the usurper Bahram Chobin, fled to the Byzantine Empire to seek asylum. Emperor Maurice of Byzantium took advantage of this opportunity to intervene in Persian internal affairs, sending troops to support Khosrow II's restoration.

v.t: Emperor Wen of Sui reorganizes Jiangnan culture
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui issued an edict to collect and collate the books and documents of Jiangnan, and ordered the abolition of the extravagant literary style of the Chen, promoting a plain and practical style. This promoted the cultural integration of north and south.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian

v.t: Reform of the fubing system in Sui
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui ordered the integration of the fubing (territorial militia) system into the state administrative system. Soldiers were no longer the private retainers of generals but were enrolled in local household registers and received land like peasants. This strengthened central control over the army and weakened the private armed forces of generals.

v.t: Empress Suiko ascends the throne in Wa (Japan)
v.s: Empress Suiko ascended the throne in Japan, the first of several female emperors in Japanese history. She appointed Prince Shōtoku as regent, who carried out a series of reforms to strengthen centralization, such as establishing the Twelve Cap Ranks and promulgating the Seventeen-Article Constitution, opening the Asuka period of reform.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian

v.t: Prince Shōtoku serves as regent, establishes the Twelve Cap Ranks
v.s: Prince Shōtoku of Japan, under the support of Empress Suiko, began to serve as regent and promoted reforms. He established the "Twelve Cap Ranks," using cap colors to distinguish official rank, breaking the hereditary clan system and selecting talents based on merit.

v.t: Sui builds the Renshou Palace
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui ordered Yang Su to build the Renshou Palace in Qizhou (present-day Fengxiang, Shaanxi) as a summer retreat. Yang Su supervised the work harshly; tens of thousands of laborers died, but the palace was extremely magnificent, reflecting the strength of the early Sui state.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian

v.t: Promulgation of the Kaihuang Code
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui formally promulgated the Kaihuang Code, consisting of twelve sections and five hundred articles. It abolished earlier harsh punishments (such as exposure and dismemberment) and established the five punishments of beating, caning, hard labor, exile, and death. The Kaihuang Code became the direct model for the Tang Code and influenced Chinese law for over a thousand years.

v.t: Emperor Wen of Sui establishes the charity granary system
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui, following the advice of Zhangsun Ping, ordered the establishment of charity granaries in all prefectures and counties. In years of good harvest, the people voluntarily contributed grain to be stored, and in times of famine, the granaries were opened for relief. This system effectively mitigated famines and was inherited and developed by the Tang dynasty.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian

v.t: Frankish kingdom becomes more divided; Merovingian dynasty declines
v.s: After the death of the Frankish king Chlothar I, the kingdom was divided among his four sons, forming Neustria, Austrasia, Burgundy, and Aquitaine. The kings fought each other for years, the power of the Merovingian dynasty waned, and the mayors of the palace (court mayors) gradually gained real power.

v.t: Sui builds the Tai Temple in Luoyang
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui ordered the construction of the Tai Temple (Imperial Ancestral Temple) in Luoyang to demonstrate the state's ritual norms. Luoyang's status as the eastern capital became increasingly important, laying the foundation for Emperor Yang of Sui's large-scale construction of the eastern capital.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian

v.t: Pope Gregory I carries out reforms
v.s: Pope Gregory I issued the "Rule for Bishops," reorganized church discipline, strengthened the management of monasteries, and promoted Gregorian chant (plainchant). He also strengthened the temporal power of the papacy, laying the foundation for the medieval papacy.

v.t: Sui economy reaches its peak
v.s: After more than a decade of recuperation, the Sui dynasty saw its state treasuries filled and its population grow substantially. According to records, in the Kaihuang era, the Tai granary in Chang'an and the Hanjiacang granary in Luoyang each stored up to ten million stones of grain. The economy reached its peak.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian

v.t: St. Augustine goes to England to preach
v.s: Pope Gregory I sent the Benedictine monk Augustine and forty monks to England to preach. Augustine was well received by King Æthelberht of Kent and succeeded in converting the king to Christianity. Augustine became the first Archbishop of Canterbury, beginning the Christianization of England.

v.t: Sui suppresses the Liao people's rebellion in the south
v.s: Li Guangshi, a chieftain of the Liao people (ancestors of the Zhuang) in the Lingnan region of Sui, launched a rebellion. Emperor Wen of Sui sent the general Wang Shiji to suppress it. Sui established prefectures and counties in the Lingnan area, strengthening central control.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian

v.t: First Sui expedition against Goguryeo fails
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui, because King Yeongyang of Goguryeo harassed the Sui border together with the Mohe people, sent Yang Liang, the Prince of Han, with 300,000 troops in a combined land and water attack on Goguryeo. However, the Sui forces were struck by typhoons and plague, suffering heavy losses and retreating without engaging in battle. This was the beginning of the Sui-Goguryeo wars, which would set the stage for Emperor Yang's three expeditions.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian

v.t: Internal split of the Eastern Turks
v.s: The Eastern Turkic rulers Tulan Qaghan and Tuli Qaghan fell into internal conflict. Emperor Wen of Sui exploited their discord, supporting Tuli Qaghan (Qimin Qaghan) to defeat Tulan. Qimin Qaghan led his tribe to submit to Sui, and Emperor Wen of Sui married a princess of the imperial house to him. Sui successfully split the Turks and eliminated the northern threat.

v.t: Foundation of Hōryū-ji in Japan
v.s: Prince Shōtoku of Japan and Empress Suiko founded Hōryū-ji in Nara as a center of Buddhist faith. Hōryū-ji is one of the oldest surviving wooden buildings in the world; its architectural style was heavily influenced by the Northern and Southern Dynasties and the Sui, and it represents the Asuka culture of Japan.

v.t: Emperor Wen of Sui in his old age becomes suspicious of the crown prince
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui, swayed by the slanders of Empress Dugu and the powerful minister Yang Su, suspected the crown prince Yang Yong of treason, deposed him as a commoner, and instead made Yang Guang (Prince of Jin) the crown prince. This decision sowed the seeds for the fall of the Sui dynasty after only two reigns.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Sui, Yang Jian

v.t: Emperor Wen of Sui deposes Yang Yong and makes Yang Guang crown prince
v.s: Emperor Wen of Sui formally issued an edict deposing Crown Prince Yang Yong and making Yang Guang the crown prince. Yang Guang, skilled in disguise, won the favor of both the emperor and Empress Dugu. This change of succession directly led to the political deterioration and excessive consumption of national strength in the later Sui period.

v.t: Japanese Prince Shōtoku sends an envoy to Sui
v.s: Prince Shōtoku of Japan sent Ono no Imoko as an envoy to the Sui court, presenting a state letter that read, "The Son of Heaven of the land where the sun rises addresses the Son of Heaven of the land where the sun sets." This displeased Emperor Wen of Sui. Nevertheless, the mission promoted cultural exchange between China and Japan, and Japan began to systematically study the institutions of the Sui and Tang dynasties.

v.t: Sui army again defeats the Turks
v.s: In the 20th year of Kaihuang (AD 600), the Sui army again defeated the Eastern Turkic forces on the northern border. The Sui employed a strategy of "using barbarians to control barbarians," supporting Tuli Qaghan to suppress Tulan Qaghan. Under Sui's military and diplomatic pressure, the Eastern Turks split. In 601, the Sui army also defeated Tardu Qaghan of the Western Turks.

v.t: Tiwanaku civilization flourishes in the Andes
v.s: The Tiwanaku civilization continued to flourish on the shores of Lake Titicaca in present-day Bolivia. Its monumental architecture, terraced agriculture, and advanced drainage systems represented the highest achievements of pre-Columbian Andean civilizations and laid the cultural foundations for the Inca Empire.

v.t: Cahokia: the central city of the Mississippian culture
v.s: From approximately 600 to 1350 AD, Cahokia (in present-day Illinois), the central city of the Mississippian culture, was the largest pre-Columbian city in North America. At its peak, its population was estimated at 10,000 to 40,000. The city was centered on Monk's Mound (30 m high, covering 6.5 hectares) and featured wooden palisades and a solar observation circle. The Mississippian trade network extended from the Great Lakes to the Gulf of Mexico. Cahokia was abandoned around 1350, possibly due to environmental degradation and social conflict.

v.t: Large-scale development of rice terraces in southern China
v.s: In the mid to late Tang dynasty (approximately 600-900 AD), large-scale development of rice terraces began in the mountainous regions of southern China. Peasants built stepped paddy fields on hillsides, channeling water for irrigation. Rice terraces not only increased arable land but also improved land-use efficiency through water and soil conservation. From the Song dynasty onward, terraces were widely adopted in Fujian, Jiangxi, Hunan, Sichuan, and other provinces. Terrace agriculture was an important technological innovation in Chinese agricultural history.

v.t: Persian windmills introduced to the Arab world
v.s: Around AD 600, the Persians invented the world's earliest windmills (panemone windmills), used for grinding grain and lifting water. These vertical-axis windmills were not affected by changes in wind direction. After conquering Persia, the Arabs mastered windmill technology and spread it throughout the Islamic world. In the 12th century, European Crusaders brought windmill technology back from Syria, and windmills became an important source of power in medieval Europe.
